     2	  "content": "# Quantitative evidence‑based comparison: Sodium‑ion vs Lithium‑ion batteries for passenger BEVs (2020–2026)\n\nExecutive summary\n- Cell gravimetric energy density (typical ranges, cell-level): sodium‑ion (Na‑ion) second‑generation: ~160–175 Wh/kg; lithium‑ion (LIB) LFP: ~140–205 Wh/kg; NMC: ~200–260+ Wh/kg (advanced NMC up to ~255–280 Wh/kg reported). [2], [1], [5], [19]\n- Cell volumetric energy density (reported examples): Na‑ion: reported full‑pouch/unigrid ~417 Wh/L and Naxtra ~356–350 Wh/L; high‑performing Li‑ion (18650) cell example up to 670 Wh/L (cell‑type dependent). [12], [11], [17]\n- Pack/system energy density (examples): Na‑ion passenger packs reported in field vehicles ~120–175 Wh/kg pack‑level claims vary by architecture; modern LFP pack/system examples ~120–162 Wh/kg (BYD Blade 2.0 ~162 Wh/kg system‑level); NMC pack ranges ~140–220 Wh/kg. [14], [15], [19], [13]\n- Cost ($/kWh): Na‑ion reported/cited cell prices around $70/kWh (CATL Q1 2026) with industry projections to $40–50/kWh by late 2020s; mature LFP cell costs cited at $40–45/kWh in China (PatSnap); LIB pack prices fell from ~$151/kWh (2022) to record lows $139/kWh (2023) and ~ $99–115/kWh in 2024–2025 depending on segment and geography. [2], [7], [20], [21], [26]\n- Cycle life and ageing: Na‑ion reported cycle life ranges broadly by platform: typical Gen‑2 claims 4,000–6,000 cycles and manufacturer claims up to 8,000–10,000+ cycles; LFP typical 3,000–4,000+ cycles; NMC typical 1,000–2,000 cycles. Calendar‑age and degradation data: Na‑ion calendar retention claims (10‑year >85–90% in some reports) and strong low‑temperature retention (>90% at –20 to –40 °C) are reported; field LIB degradation averages ~1.8–2.3% capacity loss per year in fleet studies. [2], [30], [35], [36], [32]\n- Supply and scalability: sodium raw material abundance is orders of magnitude larger than lithium (multiple cited multipliers: ~450× to ~1,000× crustal abundance; ocean/earth abundances cited), sodium‑… [line truncated]
